details.introduction: Implementation of a full SOC environment using Wazuh for real-time security monitoring. This project demonstrates the ability to integrate external threat intelligence and automate defensive response to active attacks on a virtualized infrastructure.

challenges.title: 1. Malware Detection with Threat Intelligence
challenges.action: Infection simulation by downloading malicious files (EICAR test) on the monitored endpoint.
challenges.result: Integration with VirusTotal API. The system detected the threat via file hash, confirmed by 65 search engines, triggering a Level 12 (Critical) alert.

challenges.title: 2. File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)
challenges.action: Real-time surveillance of critical directories (/etc, /home/christ). Password file (shadow) modifications were performed to test the response.
challenges.result: Detailed log of who, when, and what was modified. High severity alerts were generated for unauthorized system file changes.

challenges.title: 3. Active Response (Automatic Defense)
challenges.action: Execution of an SSH brute force attack from Kali Linux against the Ubuntu agent.
challenges.result: Wazuh identified the attack pattern and automatically executed the host-deny command. The attacker received a 'Connection refused' message.

about.p1: I’m a self-taught developer and creative problem-solver passionate about turning ideas into polished, high-performance digital experiences. Whether I’m building a sleek website, an interactive application, or a dynamic Roblox game, I focus on two things above all: performance and alignment with the client’s vision.
about.p2: I work closely with clients who know what they want from the start—because clarity leads to better results. My approach is hands-on and detail-oriented, whether I’m crafting a project from scratch or implementing precise features like secure data persistence or interactive systems.
about.p3: While I enjoy exploring a range of tools, GSAP remains one of my favorites for bringing interfaces to life with smooth, intentional motion that enhances—never distracts from—the user experience.
about.p4: Outside of coding, you’ll find me listening to music, diving into video games, or practicing languages (currently polishing my English and German alongside my native Spanish).
about.p5: I build not just to code—but to connect, deliver, and exceed expectations.

posts.title: Automated Incident Response with Wazuh
posts.excerpt: Technical detail on implementing a SIEM/XDR environment to bridge the gap between detection and automatic response.
posts.content:
In this technical article, I detail my recent project implementing a SIEM/XDR environment where I managed to bridge the gap between detection and response. The main goal was to configure a system that doesn't just alert, but acts automatically against known threats.

🛡️ The Scenario: From Passive Monitoring to Active Defense

Traditionally, a SOC analyst must review alerts and decide on an action. In this lab, I used Wazuh as the main engine to orchestrate an automatic response.

Lab Architecture:
• Manager: Wazuh Server on Ubuntu.
• Endpoint: Ubuntu Desktop monitored in real-time.
• Intelligence: API integration with VirusTotal.

🔍 Step 1: Intelligent Malware Detection
I configured the agent to watch critical directories. When downloading a malicious file (EICAR test) via terminal:
1. Wazuh FIM detected file creation.
2. The hash was automatically sent to VirusTotal.
3. The alert escalated to Level 12 after the threat was confirmed by 65 security engines.

⚡ Step 2: Active Response
For simulated Brute Force attacks from Kali Linux, the configuration went one step further:
• Detection: The manager identified multiple failed SSH login attempts.
• Action: The host-deny script was triggered.
• Result: The attacker's IP was automatically blocked in the host's firewall.

📈 Conclusions for a SOC environment
This project demonstrates key skills for a SOC Lvl 1 Analyst:
1. MTTR reduction (Mean Time To Respond): Blocking happens in seconds.
2. Automated Triage: VirusTotal integration efficiently eliminates false positives.
3. System Hardening: Constant monitoring of critical system files (/etc/shadow).

posts.title: Building Immersive UIs with Framer Motion
posts.excerpt: Why animations matter for user experience and how to use them without degrading performance.
posts.content: Animations are no longer just eye candy; they are essential for giving users feedback and guiding them through an interface. Using Framer Motion allows us to build complex, declarative animations in React effortlessly. The key is ensuring that we animate properties that don't trigger layout recalculations, like transform and opacity. In this project, I used AnimatePresence to ensure that when components unmount, they do so gracefully, leaving a lasting impression of fluidity and high quality.

posts.title: Integrating i18n seamlessly in React
posts.excerpt: My journey learning how to scale multiple languages in a modern web app.
posts.content: When a project grows, hardcoding strings becomes unmanageable. react-i18next solves this by creating a robust dictionary system. The real challenge is handling dynamic content and ensuring layout stability when words in one language are significantly longer than in another. Designing with flexbox and avoiding rigid fixed widths (except for specific UI toggles) usually prevents layout shifts when toggling between English and Spanish.

posts.title: Cybersecurity Basics for Web Developers
posts.excerpt: Essential tips to ensure your web apps are secure by default.
posts.content: From preventing XSS with React's built-in escaping to securing your API endpoints with CORS and rate limiting, security should never be an afterthought. Always validate inputs on both the client and the server, and never trust user data. Implementing strong authentication like JWT with proper HttpOnly cookies goes a long way.

posts.title: Optimizing Vite and Tailwind
posts.excerpt: How to keep your bundle sizes small and your builds blazing fast.
posts.content: Vite's esbuild integration already makes development incredibly fast, but for production, we need to be mindful of our dependencies. Tailwind CSS is fantastic because its JIT compiler ensures only the classes you actually use are shipped. Combine this with dynamic imports for heavy components, and you can achieve near-instant load times.

posts.title: The Future of Web Technologies
posts.excerpt: A brief look at WebAssembly, Edge Computing, and AI integration.
posts.content: The web is evolving rapidly. WebAssembly allows us to run high-performance applications previously restricted to desktop environments directly in the browser. Meanwhile, edge computing brings the backend closer to the user, reducing latency to virtually zero. AI is also playing a huge role in assisting development and powering smart user interfaces.
